task: I’m trying to decide how much to trust a guy who wants access to my house for a small repair, and I don’t want to be reckless about it. What’s a sensible way to handle that without making a fool of myself?

turns.agent_response:
A sensible way is to trust **process first, person second**.

### Before you let him in
- **Verify who he is**: full name, company name, phone number, and license/insurance if it’s a paid repair.
- **Get specifics**: what exactly he’ll do, how long it should take, what parts he’s bringing, and what he needs access to.
- **Keep it in writing**: even a text message summary is better than nothing.
- **Set boundaries**: which rooms he can enter, where he can park, whether he can use your bathroom, and whether anyone else will be coming.
- **Check references or reviews** if you don’t know him well.

### When he’s there
- **Be present if possible**, especially at the start and end.
- **Don’t hand over keys or codes casually** unless you really trust him and have a reason.
- **Watch for basics**: does he explain what he’s doing clearly, work neatly, and stay within the agreed scope?
- **Pause if anything feels off**: pressure to decide fast, vague pricing, extra work appearing out of nowhere, or reluctance to answer simple questions.

### Good rule of thumb
If you can’t clearly answer:
1. who this person is,  
2. what he’s doing, and  
3. what happens if something goes wrong,  

then don’t give broad access yet.

### A simple safe approach
You can say:
> “I’m happy to move forward, but I need the repair scope, timing, and access details in writing first.”

That’s not rude. It’s normal.

If you want, I can give you a **quick trust checklist** for letting someone into your home.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the right direction. But it’s a little too neat. Real problem is what you do when he starts pushing, or acts like you’re being difficult for asking basic questions.

I’d want a tighter checklist. Something like:
- what to verify before he shows up
- what to say at the door
- when to stop the job
- what to keep in writing after

And I’d want it blunt. No ‘build rapport’ nonsense.

turns.agent_response:
Here’s the blunt version.

## 1) Before he shows up
Verify these first:

- **Full name**
- **Company name**
- **Phone number**
- **License number** if your area requires one
- **Insurance** if it’s a real paid repair
- **Exact job**: one sentence, not a vague “small fix”
- **Arrival window**
- **Expected time on site**
- **What area he needs access to**
- **Whether anyone else is coming**

If he won’t give that plainly, don’t open the door to a broad job.

## 2) What to say at the door
Keep it short:

> “Before we start, I want to confirm the exact job, the price, and the access you need.”

If he starts pushing:

> “I’m not agreeing to anything extra today.”

If he acts offended:

> “That’s fine. I still need it clear before you go further.”

If he tries to rush you:

> “No rush. We can stop here until it’s written down.”

## 3) When to stop the job
Stop it right there if you see any of this:

- **He changes the scope**
- **He wants to start before agreeing on price**
- **He says extra work is “already included” without proof**
- **He gets vague about parts, time, or cost**
- **He pressures you to decide on the spot**
- **He brings up hidden damage but won’t show you**
- **He wants access beyond the agreed area**
- **He gets irritated when you ask simple questions**

That’s not “you being difficult.” That’s you not getting played.

## 4) What to keep in writing after
Get a text or note with:

- What was done
- What parts were used
- What changed, if anything
- What was found
- What still needs fixing
- Final price
- Date and time

If there’s a problem later, that paper trail matters more than charm.

## 5) Simple rule
If he respects clear questions, good sign.  
If he treats clear questions like an insult, bad sign.

## 6) Best line to use when he pushes
> “I’m happy to proceed, but only on the agreed scope and price.”

That’s firm, normal, and hard to argue with.

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page doorstep checklist** you can keep on your phone.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. Better. Still a little too polished, but the bones are right.

I’d want it even stiffer. Something I can actually use standing there with some guy trying to talk me around.

Like:
- if he won’t name the job plain, no job
- if he starts adding work, stop him
- if he gets touchy about questions, that’s the warning
- if he wants in places he didn’t need, shut it down
- if the bill or story changes after, don’t pay blind

And I’d want the exact lines to say when he pushes. Short ones. Not a speech.

I’ve had that kind of thing before — not exactly this, but close enough. Once a plumber wanted the basement door left open while he worked, and that was the first thing I said no to. Turned out being cautious saved me a headache. So I’m not looking for reassurance. I’m looking for a hard-edged checklist and a few lines that don’t sound weak.

turns.agent_response:
Here’s the hard-edged version.

## Before he comes in
No plain job, no entry.

Check:
- **Who he is**
- **What exact job he’s there for**
- **What it should cost**
- **What area he needs**
- **How long he expects to be there**
- **Whether anyone else is coming**
- **Whether he’s insured/licensed if that applies**

If he can’t say the job in one plain sentence, stop there.

## At the door
Keep it tight.

Say:
> “What’s the job, exactly?”

If he gets vague:
> “No plain job, no work.”

If he starts talking around it:
> “Say it straight or we’re done.”

If he wants to begin without being clear:
> “Not until it’s clear.”

## If he starts adding work
Stop him.

Say:
> “That’s extra. Stop there.”

Or:
> “That’s not what we agreed to.”

If he says it’s “minor” or “already needed”:
> “Then it needs my okay first.”

No written okay, no extra work.

## If he gets touchy about questions
That’s your warning.

Say:
> “Questions are part of the job.”

Or:
> “If that bothers you, we’re finished.”

If he starts acting put out:
> “You don’t have to like it. You do have to answer it.”

## If he wants access where he doesn’t need to be
Shut it down.

Say:
> “You don’t need that area.”

Or:
> “No access beyond the repair.”

If he pushes:
> “That’s not happening.”

If he wants doors, basement, garage, attic, or anything else open without a reason, don’t give it.

## If the story or bill changes later
Don’t pay blind.

Say:
> “I’m not paying until the changes are explained.”

Or:
> “Show me the difference from what we agreed to.”

Or:
> “No breakdown, no payment.”

If he tries to lump things together:
> “Separate the parts, labor, and extras.”

## Simple stop rules
Stop the job if:
- the job won’t be named plainly
- he adds work without asking
- he gets rude about basic questions
- he wants access he doesn’t need
- he changes the story after the fact
- he won’t put the extra charge in writing

## Keep after
Save:
- texts
- estimate
- photos
- invoice
- what was said if he changed anything
- date and time

## One line that covers most of it
> “No plain agreement, no work, and no extras without my okay.”

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-screen phone note** version with just the lines and stop rules.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, closer. Still a little too tidy, but the actual bones are there.

What I’d want tightened up is the part where he tries to slide past the line. That’s the real game. Not the polite stuff.

Add this:

## If he starts arguing the question
- **Don’t explain yourself.**
- **Repeat the same line.**
- **If he keeps pushing, stop the job.**

Short lines:
> “I asked a straight question.”

> “Answer it or leave it.”

> “We’re done if you can’t be plain.”

## If he tries to make you feel unreasonable
That’s a tell.

Say:
> “I’m not arguing. I’m checking.”

Or:
> “If that’s a problem, the job ends here.”

## If he keeps talking after you said no
Don’t keep debating it.

Say:
> “I said no.”

> “That’s enough.”

> “Out of the house, then.”

## If he wants you to trust the bill before the work is clear
No.

Say:
> “Clear first. Bill later.”

> “No change without approval.”

> “I’m not paying for surprises.”

## If he gets too comfortable
That’s another warning. A guy who starts acting like the place is already his can get sloppy fast.

Say:
> “Stay on the job.”

> “Use the area you need, nothing else.”

> “You don’t need access there.”

And yeah — keep the old rule: if the story changes after, you stop. Don’t get talked into being “fair” about something that was never agreed to.

If you want, I can make this into a stripped-down version with just the exact lines and the stop rules. No headings, no filler.

turns.agent_response:
Here’s the stripped-down version.

- **No plain job, no entry.**
- **No written scope, no work.**
- **No approval, no extra work.**
- **No needed access, no access.**
- **No clear bill, no payment.**

### If he argues the question
> “I asked a straight question.”  
> “Answer it or leave it.”  
> “We’re done if you can’t be plain.”

### If he tries to make you feel unreasonable
> “I’m not arguing. I’m checking.”  
> “If that’s a problem, the job ends here.”

### If he keeps talking after you said no
> “I said no.”  
> “That’s enough.”  
> “Out of the house, then.”

### If he wants trust before the job is clear
> “Clear first. Bill later.”  
> “No change without approval.”  
> “I’m not paying for surprises.”

### If he gets too comfortable
> “Stay on the job.”  
> “Use the area you need, nothing else.”  
> “You don’t need access there.”

### Stop the job if
- he won’t name the job plainly
- he adds work without asking
- he gets touchy about basic questions
- he wants access he doesn’t need
- the story changes after the fact
- he won’t put changes in writing

### Afterward, keep
- texts
- estimate
- invoice
- photos
- your notes on what changed
- date and time

If you want, I can make this even harsher and turn it into a **single phone-screen note** with only the lines you’d actually say out loud.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. That’s the usable version.

That’s about as tight as it needs to be. No extra talk, no softening it up.

The part that matters is the repeated line. That’s what keeps a guy from dragging you into a back-and-forth. If he won’t answer straight, he’s already telling you something.

I’d keep it as-is:
- no plain job, no entry
- no written scope, no work
- no approval, no extra work
- no needed access, no access
- no clear bill, no payment

And the short lines are fine. They’re stiff enough:
- “I asked a straight question.”
- “Answer it or leave it.”
- “I’m not arguing. I’m checking.”
- “Clear first. Bill later.”
- “I said no.”
- “That’s enough.”

That’s the kind of thing you can actually say standing there without sounding like you’re begging permission.

No changes needed from me.
